title: Short title, extent and commencement
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: This Act may be called the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012. It extends to the whole of India and shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may appoint by notification in the Official Gazette.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the name of the law, states that it applies throughout India, and explains that it becomes effective from the date notified by the Central Government.
examples: The POCSO Act is applicable in every State and Union Territory of India.
The Government notified the date on which the Act came into force.

faqs.question: What is the full name of this law?
faqs.answer: It is called the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012.

faqs.question: Does this Act apply throughout India?
faqs.answer: Yes, it extends to the whole of India.

correspondingSection: Not applicable

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires, various expressions including child, special court, sexual assault and other terms shall have the meanings assigned to them under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section defines important terms used throughout the POCSO Act so that they are interpreted consistently.
examples: A person below eighteen years of age is considered a child under the Act.
The term Special Court refers to a court designated for trying offences under the Act.

faqs.question: Why are definitions important?
faqs.answer: Definitions ensure that all provisions of the Act are interpreted uniformly.

faqs.question: Who is considered a child under POCSO?
faqs.answer: Any person below the age of eighteen years.

correspondingSection: Not applicable

title: Penetrative Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: A person is said to commit penetrative sexual assault if he penetrates his penis, any object, or any part of the body into the vagina, mouth, urethra or anus of a child, or manipulates any part of the child's body causing penetration, or applies his mouth to the sexual organs of the child.
simpleExplanation: This section defines what constitutes penetrative sexual assault against a child.
punishment: Punishment provided under Section 4 of the Act.
examples: Forcing sexual penetration on a child.
Using an object to sexually assault a child.

faqs.question: Does this section itself prescribe punishment?
faqs.answer: No. It defines the offence, while punishment is provided under Section 4.

faqs.question: Can objects be involved in penetrative sexual assault?
faqs.answer: Yes. The definition includes penetration using objects or body parts.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to rape and aggravated sexual offences

title: Punishment for Penetrative Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Whoever commits penetrative sexual assault shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than ten years but which may extend to imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for committing penetrative sexual assault on a child.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment of not less than 10 years, which may extend to imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: A person convicted of penetrative sexual assault may receive life imprisonment depending on the severity of the offence.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment?
faqs.answer: The minimum punishment is ten years of rigorous imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender is also liable to pay a fine.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to aggravated sexual offences

title: Aggravated Penetrative Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Penetrative sexual assault becomes aggravated when committed by specified persons such as police officers, members of armed forces, public servants, management or staff of institutions, relatives, or under circumstances specified in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section identifies situations where penetrative sexual assault is considered more serious because of the offender's position of trust, authority, or the circumstances of the offence.
punishment: Punishment provided under Section 6 of the Act.
examples: A police officer sexually assaults a child while exercising official authority.
A teacher commits penetrative sexual assault on a student.

faqs.question: Why is this called aggravated assault?
faqs.answer: Because it involves abuse of trust, authority, or particularly serious circumstances.

faqs.question: Where is the punishment specified?
faqs.answer: The punishment is provided under Section 6.

correspondingSection: BNS aggravated sexual offence provisions

title: Punishment for Aggravated Penetrative Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Whoever commits aggravated penetrative sexual assault shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than twenty years, but which may extend to imprisonment for life, meaning imprisonment for the remainder of that person's natural life, and shall also be liable to fine. In appropriate cases, the punishment may extend to death.
simpleExplanation: This section provides enhanced punishment for aggravated penetrative sexual assault because of the seriousness of the offence and the abuse of trust, authority, or vulnerability involved.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment of not less than 20 years, which may extend to imprisonment for life or death, and fine.
examples: A police officer commits aggravated penetrative sexual assault on a child.
A person in a position of trust sexually assaults a child causing severe trauma.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment under this section?
faqs.answer: The minimum punishment is 20 years of rigorous imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes, in certain aggravated cases as provided by law.

correspondingSection: BNS aggravated sexual offence provisions

title: Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Whoever, with sexual intent, touches the vagina, penis, anus or breast of a child, or makes the child touch such parts of the person or any other person, without penetration, is said to commit sexual assault.
simpleExplanation: This section defines sexual assault involving sexual touching of a child without penetration.
examples: An adult touches a child's private parts with sexual intent.
A child is forced to touch another person's private parts.

faqs.question: Is penetration required for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. Sexual assault under this section involves sexual touching without penetration.

faqs.question: Why is sexual intent important?
faqs.answer: The act must be committed with sexual intent to constitute an offence under this section.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to sexual assault

title: Punishment for Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Whoever commits sexual assault shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than three years but which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the punishment for committing sexual assault on a child.
punishment: Imprisonment of 3 to 5 years and fine.
examples: A person convicted of sexual assault on a child may be sentenced to imprisonment and fine.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment?
faqs.answer: The minimum punishment is three years of imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender is also liable to a fine.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to sexual assault

title: Aggravated Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Sexual assault becomes aggravated when committed by specified persons such as police officers, members of armed forces, public servants, relatives, teachers, staff of institutions, or under circumstances specified in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section identifies situations where sexual assault is considered more serious due to abuse of authority, trust, or vulnerable circumstances.
examples: A teacher sexually assaults a student under his supervision.
A public servant abuses official authority to commit sexual assault on a child.

faqs.question: What makes sexual assault aggravated?
faqs.answer: The involvement of a position of trust, authority, or other aggravating circumstances.

faqs.question: Where is the punishment prescribed?
faqs.answer: The punishment is prescribed under Section 10.

correspondingSection: BNS aggravated sexual offence provisions

title: Punishment for Aggravated Sexual Assault
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Whoever commits aggravated sexual assault shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than five years but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes enhanced punishment for aggravated sexual assault because of the seriousness of the circumstances involved.
punishment: Imprisonment of 5 to 7 years and fine.
examples: A teacher convicted of aggravated sexual assault on a student may receive imprisonment up to seven years.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment?
faqs.answer: The minimum punishment is five years of imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender is also liable to a fine.

correspondingSection: BNS aggravated sexual offence provisions

title: Sexual Harassment
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: A person is said to commit sexual harassment upon a child when such person, with sexual intent, utters any word or makes any sound, gesture or exhibits any object or part of body with the intention that such word, sound, gesture, object or part of body shall be heard or seen by the child; makes a child exhibit his body or any part of his body; shows any object to a child in any form or media for pornographic purposes; repeatedly follows, watches or contacts a child directly or through electronic means; threatens to use any depiction of a child's body or sexual act involving a child for pornographic purposes; or entices a child for pornographic purposes.
simpleExplanation: This section defines various acts that amount to sexual harassment of a child, including inappropriate communication, stalking, exposing a child to sexual content, and online harassment.
examples: Sending sexually explicit messages to a child.
Repeatedly stalking a child online with sexual intent.
Showing pornographic content to a child.

faqs.question: Can online harassment be covered under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. Repeated contact, stalking, or harassment through electronic means is included.

faqs.question: Is physical contact necessary for sexual harassment?
faqs.answer: No. Words, gestures, messages, or exposure to sexual content can also constitute sexual harassment.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to sexual harassment

title: Punishment for Sexual Harassment
chapter: Chapter II - Sexual Offences Against Children
officialText: Whoever commits sexual harassment upon a child shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for sexual harassment of a child.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A person sending sexually explicit messages to a child may be punished under this section.
Showing pornographic material to a child can result in imprisonment and fine.

faqs.question: What is the maximum imprisonment under this section?
faqs.answer: The maximum imprisonment is three years.

faqs.question: Can a fine also be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender may also be required to pay a fine.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to sexual harassment

title: Use of Child for Pornographic Purposes
chapter: Chapter III - Using Child for Pornographic Purposes and Punishment Therefor
officialText: A person is said to use a child for pornographic purposes if he uses a child in any form of media for the purpose of sexual gratification, including representation of the sexual organs of a child, participation of a child in real or simulated sexual acts, or indecent representation of a child.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the offence of using a child in pornographic content, whether through photographs, videos, digital media, or other forms of representation.
examples: Recording a child in sexually explicit videos.
Creating digital content depicting a child in sexual acts.
Using a child in pornographic photographs.

faqs.question: Does online content fall under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any form of media, including digital and online media, is covered.

faqs.question: Is actual sexual activity required?
faqs.answer: No. Simulated sexual acts involving a child are also covered.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to child pornography

title: Punishment for Using Child for Pornographic Purposes
chapter: Chapter III - Using Child for Pornographic Purposes and Punishment Therefor
officialText: Whoever uses a child for pornographic purposes shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to five years and shall also be liable to fine. For subsequent convictions, imprisonment may extend to seven years along with fine.
simpleExplanation: This section provides punishment for using a child in pornographic material and imposes stricter penalties for repeat offenders.
punishment: First conviction: imprisonment up to 5 years and fine. Subsequent conviction: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Publishing pornographic videos involving a child.
Distributing child sexual abuse material online.

faqs.question: Are repeat offenders punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Yes. Subsequent convictions can result in imprisonment up to seven years.

faqs.question: Is a fine mandatory?
faqs.answer: The court may also impose a fine in addition to imprisonment.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to child pornography

title: Punishment for Storage of Pornographic Material Involving a Child
chapter: Chapter III - Using Child for Pornographic Purposes and Punishment Therefor
officialText: Any person who stores or possesses pornographic material involving a child for commercial purposes or with intent to distribute, transmit, display, or share such material shall be liable to punishment as provided under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalizes the storage or possession of child pornographic material, especially when intended for distribution, transmission, or commercial use.
punishment: Imprisonment and fine as prescribed under the Act depending on the nature and purpose of possession or distribution.
examples: Storing child sexual abuse material on a computer for distribution.
Maintaining a collection of child pornographic content for commercial gain.

faqs.question: Is merely storing child pornographic material an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially when it is stored for transmission, distribution, display, or commercial purposes.

faqs.question: Does digital storage count as possession?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic storage devices and cloud storage may constitute possession.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to child sexual abuse material

title: Abetment of an Offence
chapter: Chapter IV - Abetment of and Attempt to Commit an Offence
officialText: A person abets an offence under this Act who instigates any person to commit an offence, engages in a conspiracy for the commission of an offence, or intentionally aids the commission of an offence under this Act.
simpleExplanation: This section explains that a person can be held liable not only for committing an offence but also for encouraging, planning, or helping someone else commit an offence under the POCSO Act.
examples: A person encourages another person to sexually assault a child.
Two persons plan together to exploit a child sexually.
Someone knowingly provides assistance to facilitate a POCSO offence.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished even if they did not directly commit the offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Abetting an offence is itself punishable under the Act.

faqs.question: What actions amount to abetment?
faqs.answer: Instigation, conspiracy, or intentional assistance in committing an offence.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to abetment

title: Punishment for Abetment
chapter: Chapter IV - Abetment of and Attempt to Commit an Offence
officialText: Whoever abets any offence under this Act shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for that offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who abets a POCSO offence may receive the same punishment as the person who actually commits the offence if the offence is carried out.
punishment: Same punishment as prescribed for the offence abetted.
examples: A person who helps plan a child sexual assault may receive the same punishment as the principal offender.
An individual who knowingly facilitates child exploitation may be punished similarly to the offender.

faqs.question: Can an abettor receive the same punishment as the offender?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Act allows the same punishment where the offence is committed due to the abetment.

faqs.question: Does the abettor need to be present during the offence?
faqs.answer: No. Participation through planning, encouragement, or assistance may be sufficient.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to punishment for abetment

title: Punishment for Attempt to Commit an Offence
chapter: Chapter IV - Abetment of and Attempt to Commit an Offence
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit any offence punishable under this Act or causes such an offence to be attempted and does any act towards the commission of the offence shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one-half of the imprisonment for life or one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: Even if a POCSO offence is not completed, a person can still be punished for taking substantial steps toward committing it.
punishment: Up to one-half of the maximum punishment prescribed for the completed offence, along with possible fine.
examples: A person takes concrete steps to sexually exploit a child but is stopped before the offence is completed.
An individual attempts to create child pornographic material but is intercepted.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished if the offence was not completed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting to commit a POCSO offence is punishable.

faqs.question: How is the punishment determined?
faqs.answer: It may be up to half of the punishment prescribed for the completed offence.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to attempt

title: Reporting of Offences
chapter: Chapter V - Procedure for Reporting of Cases
officialText: Any person who has apprehension that an offence under this Act is likely to be committed or has knowledge that such an offence has been committed shall provide such information to the Special Juvenile Police Unit or the local police.
simpleExplanation: This section makes reporting of POCSO offences mandatory. Anyone who knows about or suspects such an offence must inform the police.
punishment: Failure to report may attract consequences under subsequent provisions of the Act.
examples: A teacher reports suspected sexual abuse of a student to the police.
A neighbor informs authorities after learning of child exploitation.

faqs.question: Who can report a POCSO offence?
faqs.answer: Any person with knowledge or reasonable apprehension of the offence.

faqs.question: Where should the report be made?
faqs.answer: To the Special Juvenile Police Unit or the local police.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to information to police

title: Obligation of Media, Studio and Photographic Facilities to Report Cases
chapter: Chapter V - Procedure for Reporting of Cases
officialText: Any personnel of media, hotel, lodge, hospital, club, studio, photographic facility, or any similar institution who comes across material or objects sexually exploitative of a child shall provide such information to the Special Juvenile Police Unit or local police.
simpleExplanation: This section places a specific duty on certain businesses and institutions to report suspected child sexual exploitation when they encounter related material or evidence.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract penalties under the Act.
examples: A photo studio discovers images suggesting child sexual exploitation and reports them to the police.
A hotel employee informs authorities about suspected child abuse occurring on the premises.

faqs.question: Why are businesses required to report?
faqs.answer: They may encounter evidence of child exploitation during their operations.

faqs.question: Who should receive the report?
faqs.answer: The Special Juvenile Police Unit or the local police.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to reporting offences

title: Punishment for Failure to Report or Record a Case
chapter: Chapter V - Procedure for Reporting of Cases
officialText: Any person who fails to report the commission of an offence under sub-section (1) of section 19 or fails to record such offence under sub-section (2) of section 19 shall be punished with imprisonment, fine, or both as provided under this section. Where the failure is by a company or institution, the persons responsible may also be held liable.
simpleExplanation: This section penalizes individuals or institutions that fail to report POCSO offences or fail to properly record complaints as required by law.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both. Enhanced liability may apply to responsible persons in institutions.
examples: A school administrator learns of child sexual abuse but does not inform the police.
An officer refuses to record a complaint regarding a POCSO offence.

faqs.question: Can institutions be held responsible for not reporting offences?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persons in charge of institutions may be held liable.

faqs.question: Is reporting a POCSO offence mandatory?
faqs.answer: Yes. Section 19 makes reporting mandatory.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to reporting obligations

title: Punishment for False Complaint or False Information
chapter: Chapter V - Procedure for Reporting of Cases
officialText: Any person who makes a false complaint or provides false information against any person solely with the intention to humiliate, extort, threaten, or defame him shall be punished as provided under this section. A child shall not be punished for giving false information.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes malicious and knowingly false complaints made against others under the POCSO Act, while protecting children from punishment for incorrect information.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both. A child cannot be punished under this section.
examples: An adult knowingly files a fabricated complaint to harm another person's reputation.
A person falsely accuses someone to gain personal advantage.

faqs.question: Can a child be punished for making a false complaint?
faqs.answer: No. Children are specifically protected from punishment under this section.

faqs.question: What kind of false complaints are punishable?
faqs.answer: Complaints made maliciously to humiliate, threaten, extort, or defame someone.

correspondingSection: BNS provisions relating to false information

title: Procedure for Media
chapter: Chapter VI - Procedures for Media
officialText: No person shall make any report or present comments on any child from any form of media without having complete and authentic information which may have the effect of lowering the child's reputation or infringing upon the child's privacy. The identity of the child, including name, address, photograph, family details, school, neighborhood, or any other particulars that may reveal identity, shall not be disclosed except by order of the Special Court.
simpleExplanation: This section protects the privacy and identity of child victims by restricting what media organizations can publish about them.
punishment: Violation may attract imprisonment up to 6 months, fine, or both.
examples: A newspaper publishes the name of a child victim without court permission.
A television channel reveals identifying details of a child involved in a POCSO case.

faqs.question: Can the media reveal the identity of a child victim?
faqs.answer: No. Disclosure is prohibited unless specifically authorized by the Special Court.

faqs.question: Why are these restrictions important?
faqs.answer: They protect the dignity, privacy, and safety of the child.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to protection of victim identity

title: Recording of Statement of a Child
chapter: Chapter VII - Special Procedures for Recording Statement of the Child
officialText: The statement of a child shall be recorded at the residence of the child or at a place where the child ordinarily resides, or at a place of the child's choice, as far as practicable, by a woman police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector. The officer shall not be in uniform and shall ensure that the child is not detained in the police station at night.
simpleExplanation: This section requires child-friendly procedures while recording a child's statement to reduce trauma and ensure comfort and safety.
examples: A child victim's statement is recorded at home rather than at a police station.
A woman police officer records the child's statement in plain clothes.

faqs.question: Why should statements be recorded in a child-friendly environment?
faqs.answer: To minimize fear, stress, and secondary trauma to the child.

faqs.question: Can the child be kept in a police station overnight?
faqs.answer: No. The Act specifically prohibits such detention.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to recording statements

title: Recording of Statement of a Child by Magistrate
chapter: Chapter VII - Special Procedures for Recording Statement of the Child
officialText: Wherever possible, the Magistrate shall record the statement of the child under the applicable provisions of criminal procedure as soon as the offence is brought to notice. The Magistrate shall ensure that the statement is recorded in a child-friendly manner and may seek the assistance of interpreters, translators, special educators, or other experts where necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section requires Magistrates to promptly record a child's statement while ensuring the process is sensitive to the child's age, condition, and needs.
examples: A Magistrate records the statement of a child victim soon after receiving information about the offence.
A sign-language interpreter assists a child with hearing impairment during the recording process.

faqs.question: Can experts assist during statement recording?
faqs.answer: Yes. Interpreters, translators, special educators, and other experts may assist when needed.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of recording the statement early?
faqs.answer: To preserve evidence and reduce repeated questioning of the child.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to judicial recording of statements

title: Additional Provisions Regarding Recording of Statement of a Child
chapter: Chapter VII - Special Procedures for Recording Statement of the Child
officialText: The police officer or Magistrate recording the statement of the child shall ensure that the child is not exposed in any way to the accused at the time of recording the statement. Assistance of interpreters, translators, special educators, or experts may be provided where necessary. The statement shall be recorded in the language and manner best understood by the child.
simpleExplanation: This section provides additional safeguards to ensure that a child feels safe and comfortable while giving a statement and is protected from direct contact with the accused.
examples: A child gives a statement through a sign language interpreter.
The accused is kept away while the child is being questioned.

faqs.question: Can the child see the accused during statement recording?
faqs.answer: No. The child should not be exposed to the accused during the process.

faqs.question: Can experts assist the child?
faqs.answer: Yes. Interpreters, translators, special educators, and other experts may assist.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to recording statements

title: Medical Examination of a Child
chapter: Chapter VII - Special Procedures for Recording Statement of the Child
officialText: The medical examination of a child shall be conducted in accordance with child-friendly procedures. In the case of a girl child, the examination shall be conducted by a woman doctor. The parent, guardian, or another trusted person nominated by the child shall be allowed to remain present during the examination.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that medical examinations are conducted sensitively and respectfully while protecting the child's dignity and emotional well-being.
examples: A female doctor conducts the medical examination of a girl child victim.
A child's parent remains present during the medical examination.

faqs.question: Who conducts the examination of a girl child?
faqs.answer: A woman medical practitioner should conduct the examination.

faqs.question: Can a trusted person accompany the child?
faqs.answer: Yes. A parent, guardian, or trusted person may remain present.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to medical examination

title: Designation of Special Courts
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: For the purposes of providing a speedy trial, the State Government, in consultation with the Chief Justice of the High Court, shall designate for each district a Court of Session to be a Special Court to try offences under this Act.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the creation of Special Courts dedicated to handling POCSO cases quickly and efficiently.
examples: A district court is designated as a Special Court for POCSO matters.
A child sexual abuse case is tried before a Special Court.

faqs.question: Why are Special Courts established?
faqs.answer: To ensure speedy and child-sensitive trials.

faqs.question: Who designates a Special Court?
faqs.answer: The State Government in consultation with the Chief Justice of the High Court.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to Special Courts

title: Presumption as to Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: Where a person is prosecuted for committing or abetting offences under Sections 3, 5, 7, and 9 of this Act, the Special Court shall presume that such person has committed, abetted, or attempted to commit the offence unless the contrary is proved.
simpleExplanation: This section creates a legal presumption against the accused in certain serious POCSO cases, shifting the burden to the accused to rebut the presumption.
examples: An accused charged under Section 5 may need to present evidence to rebut the statutory presumption.
The Special Court begins with a presumption as provided by law.

faqs.question: Does this section automatically convict the accused?
faqs.answer: No. The accused can present evidence to rebut the presumption.

faqs.question: Why does this presumption exist?
faqs.answer: To strengthen the protection of children and address difficulties in proving such offences.

correspondingSection: BSA provisions relating to presumptions

title: Presumption of Culpable Mental State
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: In any prosecution requiring a culpable mental state on the part of the accused, the Special Court shall presume the existence of such mental state, but it shall be a defence for the accused to prove that he had no such mental state with respect to the act charged.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the court to presume that the accused had the required guilty intention or knowledge, unless the accused proves otherwise.
examples: An accused may attempt to prove lack of knowledge or intent regarding the alleged offence.
The court may presume sexual intent unless rebutted by evidence.

faqs.question: What is a culpable mental state?
faqs.answer: It refers to intention, knowledge, motive, belief, or similar mental elements required for an offence.

faqs.question: Can the accused challenge this presumption?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accused may present evidence to prove the absence of such mental state.

correspondingSection: BSA provisions relating to presumptions

title: Application of Code of Criminal Procedure to Proceedings Before a Special Court
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: Save as otherwise provided in this Act, the provisions of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 shall apply to the proceedings before a Special Court, and for the purposes of the said provisions, the Special Court shall be deemed to be a Court of Session.
simpleExplanation: This section states that normal criminal procedure rules apply to POCSO cases unless the Act provides a different procedure. A Special Court is treated as a Sessions Court for procedural purposes.
examples: Bail proceedings before a Special Court follow the criminal procedure law unless specifically modified by POCSO.
Appeal and trial procedures generally follow criminal procedure rules.

faqs.question: Does the Criminal Procedure Code apply to POCSO cases?
faqs.answer: Yes, except where the POCSO Act provides a special procedure.

faqs.question: How is a Special Court treated under this section?
faqs.answer: It is deemed to be a Court of Session for procedural purposes.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to Sessions Courts

title: Right of Child to Take Assistance of Legal Practitioner
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: The child and his or her family shall have the right to assistance from a legal practitioner of their choice for any offence under this Act. The Special Court may also appoint a support person or provide legal aid where necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that child victims and their families can receive legal assistance and representation during proceedings under the Act.
examples: A child's family engages a lawyer to assist during trial proceedings.
Legal aid is provided to a child victim who cannot afford representation.

faqs.question: Can a child victim have a lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes. The child and family have the right to legal assistance.

faqs.question: What if the family cannot afford a lawyer?
faqs.answer: Legal aid and support services may be provided.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to legal representation

title: Procedure and Powers of Special Court
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: A Special Court may take cognizance of offences under this Act directly without the accused being committed to it for trial. The Court shall ensure that the child is not repeatedly called to testify, is not exposed to the accused unnecessarily, and is treated with dignity throughout the proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section gives Special Courts special powers and requires them to conduct trials in a child-friendly manner that minimizes trauma.
examples: The Special Court takes direct cognizance of a POCSO offence.
The child is protected from repeated questioning during trial.

faqs.question: Can a Special Court directly take cognizance of a case?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may do so without committal proceedings.

faqs.question: How does the Court protect the child?
faqs.answer: By adopting child-friendly procedures and preventing unnecessary exposure to the accused.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to Sessions Court powers

title: Procedure in Case of Commission of Offence by Child and Determination of Age
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: Where any question arises regarding whether a person is a child, the Special Court shall determine the age of such person and record reasons for its determination. If the accused is found to be a child, proceedings shall be dealt with in accordance with the law relating to children in conflict with law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the court to determine age whenever it is disputed and ensures that children accused of offences are dealt with under juvenile justice laws.
examples: The Court examines school records to determine age.
A minor accused is referred to the appropriate juvenile justice process.

faqs.question: Who determines whether a person is a child?
faqs.answer: The Special Court determines age and records reasons for its decision.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused is a child?
faqs.answer: The case is handled according to juvenile justice laws.

correspondingSection: Juvenile Justice related provisions

title: Period for Recording of Evidence of Child and Disposal of Case
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: The evidence of the child shall be recorded within thirty days of the Special Court taking cognizance of the offence, and reasons for delay, if any, shall be recorded. The Special Court shall complete the trial, as far as possible, within one year from the date of taking cognizance.
simpleExplanation: This section emphasizes speedy justice by requiring prompt recording of evidence and expeditious completion of trials.
examples: The child's testimony is recorded within a month of the Court taking cognizance.
The Court aims to complete the trial within one year.

faqs.question: How quickly should a child's evidence be recorded?
faqs.answer: Within thirty days of the Court taking cognizance, as far as possible.

faqs.question: What is the target timeline for completing the trial?
faqs.answer: The trial should be completed within one year, as far as possible.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to speedy trial

title: Child Not to See Accused at Time of Testifying
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: The Special Court shall ensure that the child is not exposed in any way to the accused at the time of recording evidence, while ensuring that the accused is able to hear the statement of the child and communicate with his advocate.
simpleExplanation: This section protects child victims from direct confrontation with the accused during testimony while preserving the accused's right to a fair trial.
examples: The child gives evidence through a video link without seeing the accused.
A screen is placed between the child and the accused in the courtroom.

faqs.question: Why is the child protected from seeing the accused?
faqs.answer: To reduce trauma, fear, and intimidation during testimony.

faqs.question: Does this affect the accused's right to defend the case?
faqs.answer: No. The accused can still hear the testimony and communicate with legal counsel.

correspondingSection: BNSS witness protection provisions

title: Trials to be Conducted in Camera
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: The Special Court shall conduct the trial in camera and in the presence of the parents of the child or any other person in whom the child has trust or confidence.
simpleExplanation: This section requires POCSO trials to be conducted privately to protect the dignity and privacy of the child victim.
examples: The public is excluded from the courtroom during a POCSO trial.
A trusted guardian is allowed to remain with the child during proceedings.

faqs.question: What does an in-camera trial mean?
faqs.answer: It means the proceedings are conducted privately and not open to the public.

faqs.question: Can a trusted person accompany the child?
faqs.answer: Yes. A parent or another trusted person may remain present.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to in-camera proceedings

title: Assistance of Interpreter or Expert
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: Wherever necessary, the Special Court may take the assistance of interpreters, translators, special educators, or experts while recording evidence or conducting proceedings involving a child.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that children with language barriers, disabilities, or special needs receive appropriate assistance during legal proceedings.
examples: A sign language interpreter assists a child with hearing impairment.
A special educator helps a child with developmental disabilities understand questions.

faqs.question: Who can assist a child during proceedings?
faqs.answer: Interpreters, translators, special educators, and other experts.

faqs.question: Why are experts involved?
faqs.answer: To ensure effective communication and protect the child's interests.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to expert assistance

title: Guidelines for Child to Take Assistance of Experts
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: Subject to prescribed guidelines, the child may be provided assistance from experts, support persons, counselors, or other professionals to promote the child's well-being and facilitate participation in legal proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that children involved in POCSO cases receive professional support and counseling when needed.
examples: A counselor helps a child cope with the emotional impact of abuse.
A support person assists the child throughout the legal process.

faqs.question: Can a child receive counseling under the Act?
faqs.answer: Yes. Counseling and expert assistance may be provided.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of a support person?
faqs.answer: To help the child navigate the legal process and provide emotional support.

correspondingSection: Juvenile protection and support provisions

title: Right of Child to Take Assistance of Legal Practitioner
chapter: Chapter VIII - Special Courts
officialText: Subject to the provisions of section 301 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, the family or guardian of the child shall be entitled to the assistance of a legal practitioner of their choice.
simpleExplanation: This section reinforces the right of the child and the child's family to obtain legal representation during proceedings under the Act.
examples: The parents of a child victim engage a lawyer to represent their interests.
A legal aid lawyer assists a child whose family cannot afford representation.

faqs.question: Can the child's family choose their own lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes. The family or guardian may engage a legal practitioner of their choice.

faqs.question: Does this right apply throughout the proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Legal assistance may be obtained during proceedings under the Act.

correspondingSection: BNSS provisions relating to legal representation

title: Applicability of the Act to Other Laws
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: The provisions of this Act shall be in addition to and not in derogation of the provisions of any other law for the time being in force. In case of inconsistency, the provisions providing greater protection to the child shall prevail.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that the POCSO Act works alongside other laws. If there is a conflict, the provision that offers greater protection to the child will apply.
examples: A case may be prosecuted under both the POCSO Act and the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita where applicable.
If two laws provide different safeguards, the one more beneficial to the child is followed.

faqs.question: Does POCSO replace other criminal laws?
faqs.answer: No. It operates in addition to other applicable laws.

faqs.question: Which law applies if there is a conflict?
faqs.answer: The law that provides greater protection to the child will prevail.

correspondingSection: BNS and BNSS supplementary provisions

title: Alternative Punishment
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: Where an act or omission constitutes an offence punishable under this Act and also under any other law, then, notwithstanding anything contained in any such law, the offender found guilty of such offence shall be liable to punishment under the law which provides for punishment that is greater in degree.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that when the same act is punishable under multiple laws, the offender receives the stricter punishment available.
punishment: Punishment under the law providing the greater penalty.
examples: An offence punishable under both POCSO and another criminal law may attract the higher punishment.
The court applies the stricter sentencing provision where multiple laws overlap.

faqs.question: Why does this provision exist?
faqs.answer: To ensure offenders do not receive a lesser punishment simply because multiple laws apply.

faqs.question: Which punishment is imposed?
faqs.answer: The punishment that is greater in degree.

correspondingSection: BNS sentencing provisions

title: Act Not in Derogation of Any Other Law
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: The provisions of this Act shall be in addition to and not in derogation of any other law for the time being in force and, in case of any inconsistency, the provisions of this Act shall have overriding effect to the extent of the inconsistency.
simpleExplanation: This section gives overriding effect to the POCSO Act when its provisions conflict with other laws, ensuring stronger protection for children.
examples: A procedural conflict between POCSO and another law is resolved in favor of POCSO.
Child-friendly provisions under POCSO take precedence where inconsistent rules exist.

faqs.question: Does POCSO override conflicting laws?
faqs.answer: Yes. To the extent of inconsistency, POCSO prevails.

faqs.question: Why was this section introduced?
faqs.answer: To avoid ambiguity and ensure uniform protection for children.

correspondingSection: Special law overriding provisions

title: Public Awareness About the Act
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: The Central Government and every State Government shall take all measures to ensure that the provisions of this Act are widely publicized through media, educational institutions, training programs, and other appropriate means at regular intervals.
simpleExplanation: This section requires governments to educate the public about child protection laws and the safeguards available under the POCSO Act.
examples: Government agencies conduct awareness campaigns on child safety.
Schools organize programs explaining children's rights under POCSO.

faqs.question: Who is responsible for spreading awareness about POCSO?
faqs.answer: Both the Central and State Governments.

faqs.question: How can awareness be promoted?
faqs.answer: Through media campaigns, educational programs, and training initiatives.

correspondingSection: Government awareness provisions

title: Monitoring of Implementation of the Act
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: The National Commission for Protection of Child Rights and the State Commissions for Protection of Child Rights shall monitor the implementation of the provisions of this Act in the manner prescribed.
simpleExplanation: This section assigns oversight responsibility to national and state child rights commissions to ensure effective implementation of the Act.
examples: The National Commission reviews implementation of child protection measures.
A State Commission examines compliance with POCSO procedures.

faqs.question: Who monitors implementation of the POCSO Act?
faqs.answer: The National and State Commissions for Protection of Child Rights.

faqs.question: Why is monitoring necessary?
faqs.answer: To ensure that the Act is effectively implemented and children receive adequate protection.

correspondingSection: Child rights oversight provisions

title: Power to Make Rules
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, make rules for carrying out the provisions of this Act. Such rules may provide for matters including care and protection of children, qualifications of interpreters and experts, procedures for assistance to child victims, monitoring mechanisms, and other matters required under the Act.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes the Central Government to make detailed rules needed to effectively implement the provisions of the POCSO Act.
examples: The Government issues rules prescribing qualifications for special educators assisting child victims.
Rules are framed regarding compensation and rehabilitation of child victims.

faqs.question: Why are rules required under the Act?
faqs.answer: Rules provide practical procedures and operational details for implementing the Act.

faqs.question: Who has the authority to make these rules?
faqs.answer: The Central Government through notifications published in the Official Gazette.

correspondingSection: Delegated legislation provisions

title: Rules to be Laid Before Parliament
chapter: Chapter IX - Miscellaneous
officialText: Every rule made under this Act shall be laid before each House of Parliament as soon as may be after it is made, while Parliament is in session, for a total period of thirty days which may be comprised in one session or in two or more successive sessions. If both Houses agree to modify or annul a rule, the rule shall thereafter have effect only in the modified form or cease to have effect, as the case may be.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures parliamentary oversight over rules made under the POCSO Act by requiring them to be placed before Parliament for review.
examples: A newly framed POCSO Rule is presented before both Houses of Parliament.
Parliament modifies a rule after reviewing its provisions.

faqs.question: Why must rules be placed before Parliament?
faqs.answer: To ensure legislative oversight over executive rule-making.

faqs.question: Can Parliament change a rule made under the Act?
faqs.answer: Yes. Parliament may modify or annul the rule.

correspondingSection: Parliamentary scrutiny provisions
